sections.heading: What are XML Layouts?
sections.content: In Android's traditional View system, the user interface is defined using XML layout files stored in the res/layout/ directory. Each XML file describes a hierarchy of View and ViewGroup objects that make up a screen or a component. At runtime, Android inflates these XML files into actual View objects in memory, which are then drawn on the screen. XML layouts separate the UI design from the business logic in your Kotlin/Java code, making your code cleaner and easier to maintain.

sections.heading: Key Concepts of XML Layouts
items: Every UI element in Android is a View (e.g., TextView, Button, ImageView)
Views are grouped and arranged using ViewGroups (e.g., LinearLayout, ConstraintLayout)
Layout XML files are stored in res/layout/ and inflated by setContentView() in Activity
Each View has attributes like android:id, android:layout_width, android:layout_height
Views are referenced in Kotlin code using findViewById() or View Binding
The root element of a layout file is usually a ViewGroup that contains child Views
Layouts can be nested — a ViewGroup can contain other ViewGroups as children
Android supports multiple layout files for different screen sizes using resource qualifiers

rows.description: Expands the View to fill the full width or height of its parent container
Shrinks the View to just fit around its content (text, image, etc.)
Used in ConstraintLayout to mean 'match constraint' — fills available constrained space
Sets an exact size in density-independent pixels — avoid hardcoding where possible

sections.heading: Understanding dp, sp, and px
sections.content: Android uses density-independent units to ensure UIs look consistent across devices with different screen densities. dp (density-independent pixels) is used for all layout dimensions — margins, padding, sizes. sp (scale-independent pixels) is used exclusively for text sizes because it also respects the user's font size preference set in system settings. Never use px (raw pixels) in layouts as it will look different on every screen.

rows.use_for: All layout sizes, margins, padding, view dimensions

rows.note: Like dp but also scales with user font size preference
Does not scale — looks different on every screen density
1/72 of an inch — not recommended for Android layouts

sections.heading: Core View Attributes
items: android:id – Unique identifier for the View; reference it in code with R.id.viewName or @+id/viewName in XML
android:layout_width – Width of the View: match_parent, wrap_content, or fixed dp value
android:layout_height – Height of the View: match_parent, wrap_content, or fixed dp value
android:padding – Inner spacing between the View's content and its border (all sides)
android:paddingTop / paddingBottom / paddingStart / paddingEnd – Individual side padding
android:layout_margin – Outer spacing between the View and its neighboring Views
android:layout_marginTop / marginBottom / marginStart / marginEnd – Individual side margins
android:visibility – Controls visibility: visible, invisible (hidden but takes space), gone (hidden, no space)
android:background – Sets background color, drawable, or shape for the View
android:elevation – Sets the Z-depth shadow for material elevation effect

rows.purpose: Text input field for user to type in
Clickable button that triggers an action
Displays an image, drawable, or icon
Toggle selection widget with checked/unchecked state
Draggable slider for selecting a value in a range
Shows loading or task progress (determinate or indeterminate)
Makes content scrollable vertically when it exceeds screen height
rows.key_attributes: Can only have one direct child; use LinearLayout inside
Can only have one direct child

rows.purpose: Efficiently displays large scrollable lists or grids
rows.key_attributes: android:layoutManager, requires Adapter and LayoutManager in code

sections.heading: TextView — The Most Used View
sections.content: TextView is the most fundamental View in Android, used to display any text on screen. It supports rich text formatting, custom fonts, compound drawables (icons next to text), and spans for styled text. Many other Views like Button and EditText inherit from TextView, so understanding TextView attributes applies broadly across many components.

sections.heading: Important TextView Attributes
items: android:text – The text to display; use @string/key to reference strings.xml for localization
android:textSize – Size of the text in sp (e.g., 16sp, 20sp)
android:textColor – Color of the text (e.g., @color/black or #FF000000)
android:textStyle – bold, italic, or bold|italic
android:fontFamily – Sets the font (e.g., sans-serif, monospace, or custom font from res/font/)
android:gravity – Aligns text within the TextView: start, center, end, top, bottom
android:maxLines – Limits how many lines of text are shown
android:ellipsize – How to truncate overflowing text: end, start, middle, marquee
android:drawableStart / drawableEnd – Places an icon to the left or right of the text
android:lineSpacingExtra – Adds extra spacing between lines of text

rows.description: Multi-line text input — Enter key adds new lines
Auto-capitalizes the first letter of each sentence
Disables autocomplete suggestions from the keyboard

sections.heading: ImageView scaleType Values
items: center – Centers the image in the View without scaling
centerCrop – Scales the image to fill the View, cropping excess — maintains aspect ratio
centerInside – Scales the image down to fit inside the View if it's too large
fitCenter – Scales the image to fit within the View while maintaining aspect ratio (default)
fitXY – Stretches the image to fill the entire View — may distort the image
fitStart – Scales and aligns the image to the top-left corner
fitEnd – Scales and aligns the image to the bottom-right corner
matrix – Applies a transformation matrix for custom scaling and positioning

rows.behavior: View is shown and takes up space in the layout
View is hidden but still occupies its space in the layout
View is hidden and does not occupy any space — layout collapses

sections.heading: Accessing Views in Kotlin Code
items: findViewById<TextView>(R.id.myTextView) – The traditional way to get a reference to a View by its ID
View Binding is the modern recommended approach — generates a binding class from your layout XML
Enable View Binding in build.gradle.kts: buildFeatures { viewBinding = true }
In Activity: val binding = ActivityMainBinding.inflate(layoutInflater); setContentView(binding.root)
Access Views through binding: binding.myTextView.text = "Hello"
No need for casting or null checks — View Binding is type-safe and null-safe
Data Binding is an even more powerful option that supports two-way binding with ViewModel
Avoid storing View references in variables that outlive the Fragment's view lifecycle

sections.heading: findViewById vs View Binding vs Data Binding

rows.findviewbyid: No — can return null if ID is wrong

sections.heading: Handling Click Events
items: setOnClickListener { } – The standard Kotlin way to handle button or view clicks
binding.myButton.setOnClickListener { /* action */ } — using View Binding
Avoid using android:onClick in XML — it's fragile and not recommended in modern Android
setOnLongClickListener { true } – Triggered on long press; return true to consume the event
setOnCheckedChangeListener – For CheckBox and RadioButton state changes
addTextChangedListener – For listening to text input changes in EditText
setOnItemSelectedListener – For Spinner (dropdown) selection changes
Use lambda expressions for concise click listener syntax in Kotlin

sections.heading: Common Layout Files and Their Purpose

rows.purpose: Layout for MainActivity — the main screen of the app
Layout file for each Activity in the app
Layout file for a single item row in a RecyclerView list
Layout file for a custom dialog UI
Reusable custom view or partial layout included via <include> tag

sections.heading: Reusing Layouts with <include> and <merge>
items: <include layout="@layout/my_toolbar" /> – Embeds another layout XML file inline to avoid duplication
Use <include> for shared UI components like toolbars, headers, or footers
<merge> replaces the root element of the included layout to eliminate redundant ViewGroups
Use <merge> as the root of a layout that is always included inside another layout
<ViewStub> is a lightweight placeholder that inflates its layout lazily only when made visible
Use ViewStub for optional UI elements (e.g., error states, empty states) to improve initial load performance
Assign IDs to <include> tags to reference the included layout's root view in code

rows.reason: Flat hierarchy with no nested layouts — best for performance
Enables localization and avoids hardcoded text in XML
Centralized color management for easy theming
Consistent spacing and sizes across the app
Each extra level of nesting adds measurement and layout passes, hurting performance
Required for accessibility — screen readers use this to describe images
Type-safe, null-safe, and less boilerplate code

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: XML layouts are the foundation of Android's traditional View-based UI system. Mastering Views, their attributes, dimension units, and how to connect them to your Kotlin code using View Binding will give you the skills to build any screen layout. Focus on using the right View for each purpose, keep your layout hierarchy flat, always use resource references instead of hardcoded values, and you'll build UIs that are maintainable, accessible, and performant.
